items.q: Why can a profitable business still fail?
items.correct: Because it can run out of cash — profit counts credit sales immediately, but if cash arrives too late to pay wages and suppliers, the business becomes insolvent.
items.misconception: If a business makes a profit it cannot collapse.
items.random_wrong: Profitable businesses are automatically taken over by the government.
items.why: Students conflate profit with cash; liquidity failure is the classic cause of small business collapse in Theme 2.

items.q: Is an overdraft a good source of long-term finance?
items.correct: No — overdrafts are flexible for short-term cash gaps but carry high interest and are repayable on demand; long-term needs suit loans or share capital better.
items.misconception: An overdraft is the cheapest way to fund any business need.
items.random_wrong: Overdrafts can only be used to pay tax bills.
items.why: Students match sources of finance poorly to time-scales, a key skill in managing business activities.

items.q: Does break-even analysis give a precise, reliable answer?
items.correct: Only as a simplification — it assumes all output is sold, costs behave linearly and the price stays constant, so real outcomes often differ from the chart.
items.misconception: The break-even chart tells a business exactly how many units it will sell.
items.random_wrong: Break-even analysis applies only to airlines.
items.why: Students take the model's assumptions as facts, forgetting unsold stock, discounts and stepped fixed costs.

items.correct: Fixed costs divided by contribution per unit
items.misconception: Fixed costs divided by selling price
items.random_wrong: Total costs divided by selling price
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Fixed costs divided by contribution per unit. “Fixed costs divided by selling price” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which of these is an internal source of finance?
items.why: Correct answer: Retained profit. “Bank overdraft” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Profit can differ from cash flow because:
items.correct: Sales made on credit count toward profit before the cash actually arrives
items.misconception: Cash flow is always higher than profit
items.random_wrong: Profit measures the money in the till each day
items.why: It's Sales made on credit count toward profit before the cash actually arrives. “Cash flow is always higher than profit” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

Correct answer: Actual output minus break-even output. “Break-even output minus actual output” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Fixed costs are £40,000, selling price is £10 and variable cost is £6 per unit. What is break-even output in units?
items.why: The real figure is 10000 units. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
